source_title: Digging Deeper: A Canadian Reporter's Research Guide
author: Robert Cribb, Dean Jobb, David McKie, Fred Vallance-Jones

extracts.extract: Investigative journalism is 'in-depth reporting someone wants to keep secret, and is largely the reporter's own work,' later expanded to include 'uncovering systemic failures and misguided policies.' The core principle is that investigators seek the truth — the facts, as best as they can be determined, laid out fairly and comprehensively.

The Toronto Star analyzed the Toronto Police Service's own electronic database of arrests and made a compelling case for racial profiling. After a decade of determined reporting, the police had gone from condemning Rankin to adopting his methods. Database specialist Andrew Bailey conducted statistical analyses that were able to identify officers who were stopping and questioning racial minorities at a greater rate than their peers.
extracts.chapter: Where Daily Journalism Ends and the Investigation Begins
extracts.notes: Toronto Star racial profiling series used police arrest database; seven-year FOI battle for follow-up data

extracts.extract: Investigative interviews are conducted only after careful preparation. You will want to research prospective interviewees, find out not only what they do and what they should know, but also what they are like and how they may react in an interview. You will examine documents, notes from previous interviews, and electronic records, drawing up a list of questions or talking points before you come close to conducting an interview.

Rosner organized material in file folders and binders, each devoted to a separate topic or chapter. All the material within each binder was chronologically arranged. The key is to be able to quickly find any document or interview you need and understand its significance, as well as come to understand the bigger picture and develop a list of key points. If you are organized throughout, you will save hours, even days, of work when you reach the writing stage.

Investigative reporting differs from the daily variety in that you have the time, and the obligation, to be thorough and complete. You want to know that you have exhausted, or very nearly exhausted, the possibilities before you state something to be true. The need to be thorough applies to every aspect of an investigation. 'The need to file for deadline should never override the need to do exhaustive research,' says Rosner. 'But at a certain point the journalist must ask: has enough work been done to validate my original hypothesis?'

Investigators need to be assiduous observers. In creating a hypothesis — a statement of what the story may be — investigative journalists use methods that are near relatives of those used by scientists, police detectives, and others who seek truth. Investigators read documents with extra attention, listen just a little more carefully during interviews, and constantly assess the progress of their investigation.
extracts.chapter: The Twelve Keys — Attention and Observation

extracts.extract: Reporters working on investigative stories often become deeply involved in the work and find themselves contained in a narrow silo of facts and evidence that can blind them to what else may be going on. Constantly challenge your assumptions.

Facts stated in press releases should be checked against original sources rather than just repeated. The same goes for statements made by sources. They need to be verified by other sources or in documents. The better the information seems for your story, the more you need to be skeptical until you are certain that you have solid facts. Skepticism is at the heart of developing an investigative frame of mind.

Philip Meyer likes to talk about objectivity of method and subjectivity of purpose. The result of an investigation may be subjective — the journalist achieves her or his desire to prompt discussion and action — however, the methodology is painstakingly objective.
extracts.notes: Key epistemological distinction between objective method and subjective purpose

extracts.extract: Researching an investigative story is like assembling a jigsaw puzzle — a very hard one. Most of the pieces (the facts, people, and events) are missing. The journalist must look high and low to find the bits, and may have little sense of where they go and what the picture should look like once the puzzle is completed. Often, a single fact gleaned from one source or document will be the key to finding other records or sources that may have been overlooked. You must have the flexibility to alter the plan as you go, but a systematic approach ensures that time is not wasted.
extracts.chapter: The Nuts and Bolts of Investigations

extracts.extract: Ideally, can you reduce your inquiry to a simple question, such as 'How many people are dying from this drug?' or 'Why isn't this program working, and what must be done to fix it?'

John Tudor advises journalists to look before they leap into an investigation. They should know what they are looking for, what kind of information is available, and where it can be found. 'Success depends entirely on how well you plan.' 'Think about your research needs,' advises Robert Berkman. 'Determine why you need this information and what you plan to do with it. Write down the major points or issues to be addressed, then start making a checklist of what you will need.'

Mapping out each step in advance gives the journalist a clear sense of the sources of information to be checked, as well as other tasks. If you need to file requests under access-to-information statutes, you'll have to get this done as early as possible — it can take weeks or months to have the records released. You must also understand the context of the story. Who are the key players and how are they connected? What are the laws or rules that apply?

Steve Weinberg suggests some basic questions that should be posed at the outset of any investigation: 'How is something supposed to work, and how well is it actually working? Who wins and who loses? How?' Good journalists always try as hard to disprove their hypothesis as to prove it.
In doing research, investigators typically work from the general to the specific, from those most peripherally involved to those at the centre of the investigation.

At this point, you should revise your plan and perhaps reorder some of your priorities. You also have an opportunity here to end your investigation if it is no longer showing promise. You need to create a to-do list, typically with target dates. As tasks accumulate — and they will accumulate — you add them to the list. When they are completed, you check them off.

You must keep track of your findings in an organized way. A spreadsheet program, such as Excel, can be invaluable for keeping track of what you find during an investigation. Information can be grouped into categories using keyword fields, and sheets can later be re-sorted to bring similar information together. Links to sources, questions, and documents can also be kept in a spreadsheet. An online, cloud-based tool, such as Google Drive or Evernote, can be used to manage collaborative teams.

Whether maintained electronically or on a long piece of paper taped to the wall, a chronology allows you to keep timelines straight. A chronology should include all dates unearthed in primary documents, secondary sources, or interviews. Not only will a chronology help keep timelines straight, it can reveal inconsistencies in what interviewees say — people may not remember correctly when things happened, or they may actually lie.

Some journalists like to write rolling drafts as they investigate, because this helps them to identify what information they have, and what they still need to obtain. This can also guard against over-investigation, the gathering of material that goes beyond what is necessary.

The key to assessing tips is to check them out. Question the tipster to see if the story makes sense and is internally consistent. Once off the phone, do some basic online research. If the tip still seems credible, start digging deeper.
extracts.chapter: Where Good Ideas Come From — Tipsters

extracts.extract: You will at times discover stories by looking through a government or other database that you have obtained or built. Reporters usually have something specific in mind when they obtain data, but analysis can reveal unexpected stories. The series on car fires conducted by The Hamilton Spectator is a case in point. Looking through a database of fire calls alerted the newspaper to the frequency and timing of deliberately set vehicle fires.
extracts.chapter: Where Good Ideas Come From — Databases

extracts.extract: Documents are not only crucial to the investigative process, but they can also be its genesis. The good investigative reporter takes the time to peruse public accounts, government gazettes, city council agendas, company annual reports, and so on, in search of ideas. Watch for unusually large payments, expenditures, and other red flags.
extracts.chapter: Where Good Ideas Come From — Documents

extracts.extract: Only after other efforts to obtain witnesses and documents have failed should a journalist consider using deception. The journalist must first exhaust traditional, socially acceptable methods of investigation before resorting to the clandestine. When journalists misrepresent themselves while gathering information, their stories should explain the methods used and why they were considered necessary. By disclosing, journalists allow readers, listeners, or viewers to judge whether the ends justified the means.

Never go it alone when going undercover. Recruit a partner and rehearse your cover story — your names and relationship to each other, where you live, your phone numbers, why you are there. Two sets of eyes and ears make it far easier to witness and record what is happening. It can also be valuable to have more than one witness if the resulting story leads to litigation.

The PEI immigration students manually downloaded thousands of corporate profile reports and used a computer program to assemble them into a database. The team were able to identify hundreds of likely participants by examining the boards of directors. The students cold-called dozens of businesses and directors, confirming that most often the parties had never even met. The list of companies identified through the data analysis was shown to be almost perfectly accurate when later verified against government records released via court challenge.

We relied heavily on Google Docs to organize our research, particularly all of the factual information. We also had Google spreadsheets of people who were important to our investigation and their contact information, and timelines. We used an internal university server to store transcripts, notes, rough drafts, and our final copy. We also had paper hard copies of all of our information, which was collected and organized by a designated researcher. We used Dropbox to send copy, photos, and videos back and forth.
extracts.notes: King's College student team organization methods

extracts.extract: We immediately broke the workshop into four teams, each with specific research goals. One set out to uncover how the program worked in context of regulations; another scoured Hansard and Canada Gazette records for specific detail; the third began looking for immigrants who had participated; the fourth was on the hunt for businesses. We moved forward with the first two steps right away, but held off on step three until we could crosscheck the list of names each group came up with to ensure we wouldn't contact the same person twice.

Locating and reviewing background information is an essential step in any investigation. The goal is to know, if possible, more about your subject than the target of the investigation does. Think of media stories as briefing notes that help you grasp the complexities of a subject as you dig deeper into the story and prepare to interview experts. The goal is always the same: to learn what has already been said so you don't have to investigate what is already known.
extracts.chapter: Building a Foundation: Gathering Information Already Published or Broadcast

extracts.extract: All secondary-source information should be independently verified. An investigator must check later issues of the publication for corrections, dissenting letters to the editor, online comments, and clarifying follow-up articles, then nail down accuracy by talking to the original sources. But secondary sources can provide an invaluable starting point.

When using a website, you must be able to determine which group the site truly represents. A site might have a name that suggests it is run by an independent group with a genuine interest in an issue. A bit of research might reveal that the site is really a front for a company that makes the very product that the supposed group is championing.

With any information from social networks, careful checking is required before it is used in a broadcast or write-up. A targeted online search can help discover useful information. On Google, simply enter the name of the person you are searching, then add 'site:urlofsite' after the keywords. This will even turn up things such as whether the person has 'liked' a public Facebook post, giving you a sense of the person's preferences and connections.

Public records are the backbone of good investigative research. Each document, report, memo, inspection note, email, or disciplinary action provides a small portion of the picture. Look at it closely, and it will provide hints at the other missing pieces that need to be clustered around it to widen the image. The key lies in two equally necessary skills: perseverance and problem solving.

Think of public records as the points at which human activity intersects with the larger public interest. When companies emit toxins into the environment, that is a shared public burden. Governments account for their monitoring through documentation. One reliable starting point for any story is to think about those intersections between private and public interests — where they are, the kinds of records they create, and how to find them.

Three questions should be answered: (1) What level of government has jurisdiction over the issue — federal, provincial, or municipal? (2) What specific department would have the information? Would more than one department potentially maintain records? Ask yourself 'Who has that?' quickly followed by 'Who else has that?' (3) How are the records maintained and released? 'When something goes wrong with this particular public interest issue, where is it written down?'
extracts.chapter: Using Public Records — Where They Are

extracts.extract: As with any online search tool, the more specific you can be, using several keywords that gradually narrow your search, the more useful the results. Always use advanced search pages whenever they are available.
extracts.chapter: Using Public Records — The Federal Level

extracts.extract: Use program record listings as your own personal tip sheet on areas of interest for your journalism. Just scanning through them, while not scintillating reading, may nevertheless trigger ideas and curiosity. Learn to call records by the names officials use. Speak their language. Just perusing the list of standard records will give you ideas.

The journalistic allure of Open Government data exists in the ability to manipulate data and conduct your own analysis for the stories that matter to your readers where you are. It is the basis for comprehensive, customizable, and credible reporting.

Rick Westhead regularly uses the Merx public tendering database as a kind of tip sheet, scanning requests for proposals for hints of stories and then tracking them through time. 'I just scan them, looking for open and closed RFPs for studies and pilot projects. Then I check the timelines and come back to look at when they are supposed to be done.' He makes notes in his calendar to file access-to-information requests for completed studies.

Transcript records frame the general public debate on a particular issue, and help journalists and researchers hold officials to account over time. Speeches and positions are committed to the public record. Studying a politician's views from several months ago, or even years ago, could bear some interesting fruit when you look at what that person is saying or doing today.

A compelling analysis of political contribution records conducted by The Hamilton Spectator and the Toronto Star found political contribution limits frequently broken with questionable results. One classic method of tracking relationships is simply backgrounding people and companies involved, all the while looking for other names — of colleagues, relatives, or companies — that could appear in contribution records. Run their names through the same contribution records.

For any in-depth research, you should go beyond meeting minutes and request access to the briefing documents that were prepared for officials who attended. In some cases, these can be obtained informally; other times, you may need to submit a formal request under access legislation.

Read audit documents carefully and they provide a credible tip sheet for areas of inquiry where you can have some certainty, backed up by auditor findings, of worthwhile public-interest information to report. Older audits, even years after their release, allow you to check whether specific recommendations have been completed or whether the shortcomings documented might still exist. Careful attention to records like audit reports is a key method for breaking from the pack with enterprising stories off of the daily agenda.
It can be helpful to review budget documents even years after they are released, as a way of gauging how well a particular government met its stated fiscal goals.
extracts.chapter: What They Do — Public Accounts and Budget Documents

extracts.extract: The more potentially damaging a record, the more protective governments tend to be about releasing it. The corollary rule is that the more difficult a record is to obtain, the greater the likelihood it contains information of genuine interest and concern.

Whenever you learn of a food recall from a specific company, it's worth checking the CFIA's site to see if the food producer has a history of similar problems that point to a larger concern or pattern. It is essential to consider the jurisdictional sharing of powers and supplement federal data with records from individual provincial ministries.

CTV reporters Bob Fife and Philip Ling found answers to the XL Foods beef recall by using US Department of Agriculture Food Safety and Inspection Service records, after Canadian officials refused to explain what was happening. For more than a decade, US authorities had detailed deficiencies at XL Foods plants, including sloppy record-keeping and equipment held together by duct tape.
extracts.notes: When domestic officials refuse transparency, check foreign regulators

extracts.extract: It is important to understand the background of non-governmental organizations to recognize their ideological predispositions. Unlike opposition parties, the biases of special-interest groups and think tanks are not immediately obvious without some prior research. Studies on poverty or hospital wait times published by the Fraser Institute must be considered in light of the think tank's right-leaning stance.

The process of researching public records is all about asking the right questions and learning how systems operate. It isn't until you understand how the paper trail works in a particular public agency or department that you understand the kinds of records that might exist and what they can really tell you.

Note that there may be value in running the names of lobbyists, their partners, spouses, etc., through the election contribution database to see if they support specific politicians or parties. You can also thoroughly background lobbying activities by company name — search for the corporate name and a list of lobbyists appear, both current and inactive. Dig deeper and you'll find monthly communication reports detailing written or oral communications between the company and government institutions.

It's also important to note that the names of those on corporate filings are not always the people running the company. Those who wish to remain anonymous can list colleagues, partners, and others in corporate filings while continuing to exercise control from behind the scenes. Getting at the real influence behind a company doesn't always end with a corporate search.

You establish a reputation. You're creating the landscape for the documents to come your way. After spending two months writing the story, Bousquet's investigation into a mayor's mishandling of an estate was built on public records — property records, probate court files — supplemented by confidential sources who provided bank records proving the embezzlement.

Robert Cribb analyzed the charitable returns of six Canadian National Hockey League team charities and found that despite stated missions of helping disadvantaged children, most spent between 40 and 65 per cent of their donations — and as much as 80 per cent in one year — to fundraising and administration. Whatever the journalist's beat or area of interest, it's worth digging into the local or national charities in that space.

Three reporters from The Hamilton Spectator, the Toronto Star, and The Record teamed to analyze more than 50,000 aviation incidents, revealing troubling safety patterns including hundreds of potentially fatal mistakes by pilots and air-traffic controllers. As a result of the Spectator's four-year pursuit of the incident data, Transport Canada changed its policies to allow for the public release of the incident records.
extracts.notes: Four-year data pursuit; collaboration across three newsrooms

extracts.extract: The Hamilton Spectator reporter tracked down a food-poisoning victim by taking two pieces of information from a redacted FOI document — the date of a banquet and a reference to the town of Grimsby — then searching through a year's worth of local weekly newspaper issues on microfilm to find a matching wedding announcement, identifying and confirming the correct wedding.
extracts.notes: Demonstrates using fragments from FOI docs to reconstruct identities

extracts.extract: A problem — be it racial profiling, dirty restaurants, or elder abuse — uncovered in one city may exist in yours, too. Localizing stories from other geographic areas is a productive approach for generating investigative ideas.

The information tends to build on information. Following up on leads, the names of individuals, recollections, and details one source has said, you will likely want to speak to people involved to hear their story and put a human face on the information. The key is to be able to say, assuredly, 'This is what happened' or 'This is who did it.'

While most professional regulatory bodies have online directories of members, those sites rarely include disciplinary records. You have to request records, and may come up against restrictions on what can be published. Note that disciplinary hearings and decisions represent only the tip of the iceberg in terms of complaints by clients, patients, and so on.
extracts.chapter: What They Do — Professional Licensing and Disciplinary Records

extracts.extract: The first in-depth examination of proactive travel disclosures was a 2008 Toronto Star investigation that analyzed about 60,000 travel expenses from federal ministers, political staff, and senior public servants. The records have led to a steady stream of accountability stories ever since for eagle-eyed journalists.
extracts.chapter: Using Public Records — Transparency and Proactive Disclosure

extracts.extract: Dig into your provincial government's website and you're bound to discover interesting areas of inquiry you may never have considered. Also look for stories in unexpected places. Forms are also a hint of the kinds of databases that exist within the ministry, since the information filled out on those documents almost certainly ends up in a computer hard drive.

Reading government statements that were made some time ago can help you trace a government's evolving position, bringing you a far greater understanding of public-policy decisions and their implications.
